task: I just picked up my car after a tire replacement, and something about the paperwork/specs doesn’t look right to me. Can you help me figure out what to check and how to word a calm follow-up if I need them to fix it?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — best approach is to verify the **paperwork against the actual car** before calling them back.

## What to check first
Compare the invoice/work order to:
- tire size
- load index and speed rating
- brand/model
- quantity replaced
- DOT date codes if listed
- alignment or balancing charges if any
- TPMS service charges if any
- torque / recheck notes if included

Then check the car itself:
- all four tire sizes match what’s supposed to be on the vehicle
- the replaced tire(s) match the invoice
- pressures look correct
- no TPMS light
- no obvious damage or missing valve caps
- lug nuts appear seated normally

## Common things that look “off”
- wrong tire size on the invoice
- a different tire model than what you asked for
- only one tire replaced when you were quoted more
- no note about balancing, TPMS, or alignment when it should have been included
- a speed/load rating lower than required
- mismatched tire on an axle where matching matters

## If something seems wrong
Don’t accuse them yet. Start with:
- “I’m reviewing the invoice and wanted to clarify one item.”
- “The tire/specs on the paperwork don’t seem to match what I expected.”
- “Can you help me verify exactly what was installed?”

## Calm follow-up wording
You can say:

> Hi, I just picked up the car and I’m comparing the paperwork to the tire specs. I noticed [specific issue]. Can you please verify what was installed and confirm it matches the vehicle requirements? If there was a mistake, I’d like to get it corrected.

If you want it a little firmer:

> Hi, I noticed the tire/spec information on the invoice doesn’t look right compared with what was requested and what the vehicle needs. Can you check the work order and let me know how you’d like to correct it?

## What to ask them to confirm
- exact tire size installed
- load index / speed rating
- whether the correct tire was mounted on the correct axle
- whether TPMS was reset or relearned if needed
- whether balancing and torque were completed

## Good rule
If the paperwork and the vehicle don’t agree, ask for:
- the **work order details**
- the **installed tire specs**
- the **reason for any substitution**

If you want, paste the tire size/specs from the invoice and I can help you spot what looks off before you call them.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is the right kind of check. But I’d want a little more specificity before I call them.

What I’d verify, in order:
- invoice tire size vs what’s actually on the car
- load index / speed rating
- whether they installed the exact brand/model quoted, or substituted something else
- quantity replaced
- whether the right tire went on the right corner/axle
- any TPMS reset or relearn note
- torque / recheck note

If the spec looks wrong, I’d also check the sidewall on the tire itself, not just the paper. Paperwork gets copied wrong more often than people admit.

A calm follow-up I’d use:

“Hi, I’m reviewing the invoice and the tire specs don’t look like they match what was requested. Can you confirm the exact tire size, load index, and speed rating that were installed? If there was a substitution, I’d like to understand why and have it corrected if needed.”

If you want to be a little firmer without sounding heated:

“Hi, I compared the invoice with the tire on the vehicle, and I think there may be a mismatch in the specs. Can you check the work order and confirm what was actually installed? If something was entered or installed incorrectly, I’d like to get it fixed.”

If you’ve got the invoice text, I can help you spot the specific mismatch fast.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s the tighter version I’d use before calling them.

## What to verify, in order
1. **Invoice tire size vs actual tire sidewall**
2. **Load index / speed rating**
3. **Exact brand/model quoted vs installed**
4. **Quantity replaced**
5. **Correct tire on correct corner/axle**
6. **TPMS reset/relearn note**
7. **Torque / recheck note**
8. **Balancing / alignment / rotation notes if expected**

## What to check on the tire itself
Look at the sidewall for:
- size
- load index
- speed rating
- brand/model
- DOT date code if you want to verify age

If the paperwork looks off, the tire sidewall is the reality check.

## What would count as a red flag
- size on invoice doesn’t match sidewall
- load/speed rating is lower than required
- different tire model installed than quoted without explanation
- only part of the job was done when more was promised
- no TPMS note when the car needs a relearn
- no torque/recheck note after wheel removal

## Calm follow-up script
You can say:

> Hi, I’m reviewing the invoice and comparing it to the tire on the vehicle. I noticed a possible mismatch in the tire specs. Can you confirm the exact size, load index, speed rating, and brand/model that were installed? If there was a substitution, I’d like to understand it and make sure it’s corrected if needed.

If you want it a little firmer:

> Hi, I compared the invoice to the tire sidewall and I think there may be a spec mismatch. Can you check the work order and confirm what was actually installed on the car? If anything was entered or installed incorrectly, I’d like to get it fixed.

## One good rule
If the **invoice and the sidewall disagree**, trust the sidewall and ask them to explain the paperwork.

If you want, paste the invoice text or tire size and I can help you spot the mismatch before you call.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the level of specificity I wanted. Sidewall first, invoice second if they disagree.

The only thing I’d add is to check whether the tire was mounted on the correct axle if they replaced just one. On some setups, mismatching an axle matters more than people think.

That follow-up script is fine. I’d probably use the firmer one if I already saw the mismatch myself:

“Hi, I compared the invoice to the tire sidewall and I think there may be a spec mismatch. Can you check the work order and confirm what was actually installed on the car? If anything was entered or installed incorrectly, I’d like to get it fixed.”

Clean, not hostile. Good enough to send.
